key_insight: The paper proposes a modular calculus that separates optimizer geometry from nongeometric mechanisms and proves that full positive-definite geometry can express exactly the strict descent directions away from critical points.
engineering_relevance: It gives optimizer designers a diagnostic framework for auditing which gradient directions are reachable under budget, state, and operator constraints, rather than ranking optimizers by a single score.
method: Defines a geometric module as a positive cometric family mapping covectors to descent directions.
Introduces nongeometric modules for information, memory, control, constraints, noise, target changes, and discretization.
Proves direction-expressivity and residual-complexity results for full, diagonal, and block geometries.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports diagnostic prototypes: a high-information full-metric probe solves deterministic quadratic benchmarks to numerical precision, and a Muon-style PyTorch candidate provides small-scale evidence for the calculus.
watch_item: The framework is primarily theoretical and benchmark-language oriented, so its practical value may depend on whether the abstract residual diagnostics transfer to large-scale training.
paper_title: Geometric--Nongeometric Optimizer Calculus: A Modular Language for Reachable Gradient Methods
